rules.condition: WHEN working with piston pumps in continuous-flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use piston pumps for high-pressure regimes up to ~140 bar
rules.reasoning: Piston pumps work at high-pressure regimes and are a popular choice for continuous-flow systems

rules.condition: WHEN requiring even higher pressures than standard piston pumps
rules.recommendation: Use plunger pumps made from steel, stainless steel, nickel-molybdenum alloys (Hastelloy), or ceramics for pressures up to 2,070 bar
rules.reasoning: These materials provide good resistance against corrosive chemical reagents and can achieve much higher pressures

rules.condition: WHEN experiencing pulsating flow with piston pumps
rules.recommendation: For very low flow rates, prefer syringe pumps over piston pumps
rules.reasoning: Piston pumps generate pulsating flow, and pulsation may still be problematic at low flow rates despite employing two pistons to smooth the flow

rules.condition: WHEN processing highly viscous materials
rules.recommendation: Avoid piston/plunger pumps as processing is difficult
rules.reasoning: The mechanism of piston/plunger pumps makes processing of highly viscous materials difficult

rules.condition: WHEN processing slurries or suspensions
rules.recommendation: Avoid piston/plunger pumps as processing is difficult or impossible
rules.reasoning: Particulates impede the operation of most nonreturn check valves in piston/plunger pumps

rules.condition: WHEN using piston/plunger pumps
rules.recommendation: Avoid airlocks at all costs as they can disrupt flow
rules.reasoning: Airlocks in piston/plunger pumps can disrupt the flow and should be avoided

rules.condition: WHEN handling gas-liquid mixtures
rules.recommendation: Use diaphragm pumps with reciprocating flexing membrane and Teflon membranes for aggressive chemicals
rules.reasoning: Diaphragm pumps are suitable for gas-liquid mixtures and Teflon membranes provide chemical resistance
rules.exceptions: Working pressure is typically lower than piston/plunger pumps due to compressed air drive

rules.condition: WHEN using gear pumps for continuous-flow processes
rules.recommendation: Use gear pumps for pressures up to ~200 bar, particularly suitable for viscous fluids
rules.reasoning: Gear pumps can work at pressures up to ~200 bar and are particularly suitable for transfer of viscous fluids

rules.condition: WHEN working with highly corrosive liquids
rules.recommendation: Design gear pumps to withstand highly corrosive liquids
rules.reasoning: Gear pumps can be designed to withstand highly corrosive liquids

rules.condition: WHEN handling slurries and viscous liquids
rules.recommendation: Use peristaltic pumps as they are particularly suitable for these applications
rules.reasoning: Peristaltic pump closely resembles gastrointestinal movement and is particularly suitable for handling slurries and viscous liquids

rules.condition: WHEN needing versatile chemical compatibility
rules.recommendation: Use peristaltic pumps as they offer the most versatile chemical compatibility
rules.reasoning: Only the tubing contacts the fluid, and tubing is available in wide range of materials and dimensions, making changing between applications trivial and cheap

rules.condition: WHEN pressure requirements are low
rules.recommendation: Consider peristaltic pumps, but note they deliver quite low pressure (<10 bar) compared to other pump types
rules.reasoning: The pressure delivered by peristaltic pumps is quite low in comparison with other pumps described

rules.condition: WHEN controlling fluid flow to set points in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use mass-flow controllers (MFC) with thermal sensors consisting of two resistance thermometers wrapped around a small tube
rules.reasoning: MFCs measure and control flow of liquids and gases by detecting temperature difference created when gas flows through heated tube
rules.quantitative: Temperature range 2-4°C for heating the tube
rules.exceptions: Do not confuse with BPRs (back-pressure regulators)

rules.condition: WHEN needing precise pressure maintenance under controlled fluid flow conditions
rules.recommendation: Use back-pressure regulators (BPRs) which are nonreturn valves for upstream pressure control
rules.reasoning: BPRs are essential for gas-liquid reactions, gas evolution during reaction, and maintaining volatile reactants in liquid phase when reaction temperature exceeds boiling point

rules.condition: WHEN needing simple and cheap pressure control for laboratory-scale continuous-flow operations
rules.recommendation: Use fixed or static BPRs with nonreturn check valve with fixed set point
rules.reasoning: Being the simplest and cheapest type, they maintain upstream pressure just below the limit by opening to release excess pressure

rules.condition: WHEN needing manual or electronic control of working pressures
rules.recommendation: Use variable or adjustable BPRs with spring-loaded pressure relief valves or dome-loaded devices
rules.reasoning: These allow varying the pressure at which the valve opens and offer working pressures up to ~1,034 bar

rules.condition: WHEN operating back pressure regulators (BPR)
rules.recommendation: Operate at pressures up to 689 bar (10,000 psi)
rules.reasoning: BPRs can handle high pressures and can be constructed entirely using polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) or Hastelloy for excellent chemical compatibility

rules.condition: WHEN constructing back pressure regulators for chemical compatibility
rules.recommendation: Use polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) or Hastelloy materials
rules.reasoning: These materials offer excellent chemical compatibility for flow chemistry applications

rules.condition: WHEN heating flow reactors with water bath
rules.recommendation: Use water bath temperatures up to 95°C
rules.reasoning: Water baths provide common approach for heating reactor tubes, coils, or chips

rules.condition: WHEN heating flow reactors with oil bath
rules.recommendation: Use oil bath temperatures up to ~250°C for precise temperature control
rules.reasoning: Oil baths can achieve higher temperatures than water baths while maintaining precise control with thermostats

rules.condition: WHEN cooling flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Use ice-salt mixtures down to -20°C, or liquid N2/dry ice with solvents down to -78°C
rules.reasoning: These cooling methods provide effective temperature control for low-temperature reactions
rules.quantitative: down to -20°C (ice-salt), down to -78°C (liquid N2/dry ice)

rules.condition: WHEN requiring cryostatic conditions for experiments lasting several hours
rules.recommendation: Use electronically controlled refrigerated circulators (e.g., Julabo, Huber, Heidolph) down to -95°C
rules.reasoning: These provide consistent cooling at cryostatic conditions and are good for in-house reactor setups despite being bulky and requiring circulatory cooling liquid

rules.condition: WHEN using HPLC column thermostats for temperature control
rules.recommendation: Achieve temperature range from -10 to 200°C without cooling fluids using Peltier elements
rules.reasoning: HPLC column thermostats allow very precise temperature maintenance and temperature programming (ramping and holding times)

rules.condition: WHEN using GC ovens for heating/cooling
rules.recommendation: Utilize temperature range from -80 to 450°C depending on manufacturer
rules.reasoning: GC ovens offer much wider temperature range making them the heating/cooling device of choice for in-house continuous-flow reactors, though they have sizeable footprint
rules.exceptions: Occupy significant bench space in synthetic laboratory

rules.condition: WHEN designing reactor connections and tubing
rules.recommendation: Consider thermal resistance of materials to minimize temperature gradients and excessive heat loss through reactor walls
rules.reasoning: Important to maintain consistent temperature throughout the reactor system

rules.condition: WHEN working with microreactors (<1 mm, typically μm scale)
rules.recommendation: Rely on rapid diffusion for mixing due to high surface-to-volume ratio
rules.reasoning: In microreactors, the high surface-to-volume ratio enables mixing to be achieved through rapid diffusion

rules.condition: WHEN working with larger reaction channels where diffusion is less efficient
rules.recommendation: Deploy static (passive) or dynamic (active) mixers, particularly for multiphase reaction mixtures
rules.reasoning: In larger reaction channels, mixing by diffusion is generally less efficient, particularly for multiphase reaction mixtures

rules.condition: WHEN choosing between static and dynamic mixers
rules.recommendation: Use static mixers for energy efficiency and absence of moving parts; use dynamic mixers for highly turbulent flow and longer residence times
rules.reasoning: Static mixers are more energy efficient and safer with no electrical components or moving parts. Dynamic mixers achieve highly turbulent flow (high Reynolds numbers) preferred for longer residence times or highly immiscible liquids

rules.condition: WHEN using Y-piece, T-piece, or cross-piece mixers
rules.recommendation: Mix solutions at angles of 60°, 90° (cross-flow), or 180° (opposing flow) depending on application
rules.reasoning: The extent of mixing depends on dimension, speed (flow rate), and direction of merging fluids

rules.condition: WHEN dealing with miscible fluids with similar viscosities for undemanding applications
rules.recommendation: Use simple union pieces (Y-piece, T-piece, cross-piece) for effective mixing
rules.reasoning: These union pieces can create effective mixing for relatively undemanding applications

rules.condition: WHEN reactions are biphasic/multiphasic or only slow flow rates can be used
rules.recommendation: Deploy additional mixers before the reactor or incorporate mixers into the reaction chamber
rules.reasoning: More demanding reactions require additional mixing to ensure good mixing between reactants or immiscible phases during reaction

rules.condition: WHEN using inert packings for mixing enhancement
rules.recommendation: Pack reactor internal volume with sand, silica gel, silica carbide, or glass beads
rules.reasoning: Packing reduces reactor volume leading to shorter residence time, so void volume determination is important for residence time calculations
rules.exceptions: Important to determine void volume for residence time calculations

rules.condition: WHEN using packed-bed reactors
rules.recommendation: Determine void volume experimentally by weighing reactor with dry bed and again when saturated with liquid of known density
rules.reasoning: Packing reduces reactor volume, leading to shorter residence time, making void volume determination crucial for accurate residence time calculations

rules.condition: WHEN dealing with very fine particles in packed beds
rules.recommendation: Pass material through a sieve to remove fine particles to avoid clogging
rules.reasoning: Very fine particles can cause clogging or unacceptable pressure buildup in the system

rules.condition: WHEN selecting materials for exothermic reactions or fast flow rates
rules.recommendation: Use metal (titanium, nickel), plastic (PTFE), or ceramic foams as alternatives to packed bed reactors
rules.reasoning: The porosity and rigidity of these foams can be optimized to afford high surface area to volume for efficient heat transfer while preventing unacceptable pressure drops

rules.condition: WHEN using structured mixers for precise flow control
rules.recommendation: Place structured static mixers (wave, spiral, baffles) directly inside reactor tube to achieve excellent radial mixing while maintaining plug-like axial flow profile
rules.reasoning: Structured mixers provide more precise control of flow patterns compared to particles and foams

rules.condition: WHEN using gas-liquid contactors (bubble column reactors)
rules.recommendation: Use porous media (frit or microfiltration membrane) made of titanium, stainless steel, or PTFE to generate small gas bubbles
rules.reasoning: Creates large interfacial contact between gas and liquid phases, providing good mixing and heat transfer with precise control of bubble size through pore size and gas flow rate

rules.condition: WHEN achieving turbulent flows in microchannels is impossible
rules.recommendation: Modify reaction channel structure into twisted pipes to induce radial mixing through centrifugal forces
rules.reasoning: As fluid flows around bends, pressure differential from centrifugal forces causes molecules to move toward outside of bend, creating radial mixing
rules.quantitative: high Reynolds numbers required for turbulent flow

rules.condition: WHEN designing split-and-recombine (SAR) systems
rules.recommendation: Continually separate and merge reaction mixture to achieve chaotic flow
rules.reasoning: This approach enhances radial mixing of viscous liquids while allowing high surface area for effective heat transfer

rules.condition: WHEN designing continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTRs) for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use small internal volumes (e.g., 2 mL) to achieve efficient mixing and maintain plug flow-like processing
rules.reasoning: Small internal volumes allow efficient mixing to be achieved and create a more uniform, plug flow-like processing environment
rules.quantitative: 2 mL internal volume for lab-scale CSTR

rules.condition: WHEN controlling mixing in continuous stirred tank reactors
rules.recommendation: Control mixing by varying revolutions per minute (RPM) of the magnetic stir bar
rules.reasoning: Magnetic stirring provides controllable agitation similar to flask-based synthesis

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up CSTR operations for better residence time distribution
rules.recommendation: Use a larger number of lower volume reactors instead of a single CSTR of the same combined volume
rules.reasoning: Multiple smaller reactors give a better residence time distribution than a single large CSTR
rules.quantitative: Multiple units vs single combined volume

rules.condition: WHEN implementing oscillatory flow for enhanced mixing
rules.recommendation: Use time-pulsating flow with pressure gradients to achieve back-mixing and introduce constrictions (baffle disks with orifices) along the tube
rules.reasoning: Oscillating pump creates eddies on upstroke and downstroke, creating highly turbulent flow for enhanced mixing
rules.quantitative: Time-pulsating flow with baffle disk orifices

rules.condition: WHEN fabricating microfluidic reactors
rules.recommendation: Use transparent materials such as acrylic, glass, silicon, or polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) for micro-structured channels
rules.reasoning: These materials allow for etching or molding of microchannels and provide optical transparency for monitoring

rules.condition: WHEN designing microfluidic reactors for multiple operations
rules.recommendation: Integrate multiple units of operation (mixing, heating/cooling, quenching, product extraction) in uninterrupted continuous flow using 'lab on a chip' technique
rules.reasoning: Integration allows for complete reaction processes without interruption and efficient use of space

rules.condition: WHEN mixing in narrow microchannels
rules.recommendation: Utilize diffusion in laminar flow for efficient mixing, which cannot be achieved in larger (meso-scale) channels
rules.reasoning: Narrow channels enable efficient mixing through diffusion in laminar flow regime
rules.exceptions: Larger channels cannot achieve this type of mixing

rules.condition: WHEN designing microreactors for small-scale applications
rules.recommendation: Use internal volumes as small as picoliters (pL) for applications with very limited material quantities
rules.reasoning: Miniaturized features are highly desirable for bioanalytical sciences, bioassays, and synthetic chemistry optimization with limited material

rules.condition: WHEN designing microreactors for heat transfer
rules.recommendation: Utilize the large inner surface area-to-volume ratio (several tens of thousands m² m⁻³) to achieve very high heat-transfer coefficients of up to 20-30,000 W m⁻² K⁻¹
rules.reasoning: High surface area to volume ratio provides very efficient heating/cooling for safe performance of highly exothermic reactions
rules.quantitative: Surface area-to-volume ratio: tens of thousands m² m⁻³; Heat transfer coefficients: 20-30,000 W m⁻² K⁻¹

rules.condition: WHEN performing highly exothermic reactions with hazardous and reactive intermediates
rules.recommendation: Use microreactors with high heat-transfer coefficients to perform reactions safely
rules.reasoning: Very efficient heating/cooling allows safe handling of hazardous and reactive intermediates in exothermic reactions
rules.quantitative: Heat transfer coefficients up to 20-30,000 W m⁻² K⁻¹

rules.condition: WHEN conducting gas-liquid biphasic reactions requiring high surface area contact
rules.recommendation: Use annular flow regime to afford high surface area contact between gaseous and liquid phases within a microchannel
rules.reasoning: Annular flow provides maximum interfacial contact area beneficial for mass-transfer limited reactions
rules.exceptions: High gas flow rate required to maintain annular flow, which may not be practical for long residence time reactions

rules.condition: WHEN conducting biphasic reactions in microreactors with long residence time requirements
rules.recommendation: Use segmented flow instead of annular flow for biphased reactions
rules.reasoning: Segmented flow is often favored because annular flow requires high gas flow rates that may not be practical for long residence times
rules.exceptions: When high surface area contact is more important than residence time

rules.condition: WHEN controlling contact between two phases in segmented flow
rules.recommendation: Control contact between phases by adjusting the lengths of the gas and liquid slugs
rules.reasoning: Slug length adjustment provides control over phase contact time and mixing characteristics

rules.condition: WHEN designing liquid-liquid segmented flow microchannels
rules.recommendation: Ensure the cross section of each segment is smaller than its length to avoid emulsion formation
rules.reasoning: Proper geometry prevents unwanted emulsion formation during liquid-liquid segmented flow
rules.quantitative: Cross section < length for each segment

rules.condition: WHEN designing liquid-liquid segmented flow microchannels
rules.recommendation: Consider the material used in microchannel construction as it affects interfacial tension and surface energies between the liquid and reactor wall
rules.reasoning: Material choice impacts the physics of segmented flow through interfacial tension and surface energy effects
rules.quantitative: Material-dependent interfacial tension and surface energies

rules.condition: WHEN using PDMS chip reactors
rules.recommendation: Keep pressure drop below 2-3 bar
rules.reasoning: Higher pressure drops can cause strain on reactor walls and fittings, which can be dangerous, and affects gas solubility
rules.quantitative: ≤ 2-3 bar pressure drop for PDMS chip reactors

rules.condition: WHEN reactions involve highly viscous liquids and high flow rates in narrow channels
rules.recommendation: Monitor for problematic pressure drops and implement pressure monitoring
rules.reasoning: Narrow channels are prone to fouling and blockages which can cause dangerous pressure buildups

rules.condition: WHEN there is potential for blockage events in tubular reactors
rules.recommendation: Monitor reactor pressure and configure system to automatically shut down if blockage is detected
rules.reasoning: Clogging can cause dangerous pressure buildups in the system

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up microreactor processes
rules.recommendation: Use reactor stacking (scaling-out) or parallelization (numbering up) of microreactors rather than simple scale-up
rules.reasoning: Achieving even flow distributions between reactors is challenging but necessary for consistent results

rules.condition: WHEN selecting reactor materials for cost-effectiveness
rules.recommendation: Use glass reactors as the cheapest and most popular choice
rules.reasoning: Although other materials like polymers, metals, and ceramics are available, glass offers the best cost-performance ratio
rules.exceptions: When chemical compatibility or extreme conditions require other materials

rules.condition: WHEN using coil reactors with internal diameters ≤2 mm
rules.recommendation: No additional mixers are needed for homogeneous reactions
rules.reasoning: Small internal diameters provide sufficient mixing through the coil geometry

rules.condition: WHEN using 1/16" stainless steel tubing
rules.recommendation: Can withstand pressures up to 12,000 psi (827 bar) depending on wall thickness
rules.reasoning: Stainless steel has high pressure tolerance for high-pressure reactions
rules.quantitative: Up to 12,000 psi (827 bar) for 1/16" stainless steel tubing

rules.condition: WHEN performing reactions at elevated temperatures up to 500°C
rules.recommendation: Use metal coils for good heat transfer with high surface-to-volume ratio
rules.reasoning: Metal coils provide excellent heat transfer capabilities at high temperatures

rules.condition: WHEN using chemically aggressive reagents with extreme pH values
rules.recommendation: Prefer polymer tubings over metal tubings to eliminate leaching of catalytic metals
rules.reasoning: Metal leaching can influence reaction outcomes and contaminate products
rules.exceptions: When high temperature tolerance is required

rules.condition: WHEN using PEEK tubing (1/16")
rules.recommendation: Can withstand up to 6,000 psi (413 bar) pressure but limited to 143°C due to glass transition temperature
rules.reasoning: PEEK offers good chemical and pressure tolerance but has temperature limitations

rules.condition: WHEN designing packed-bed reactors for heterogeneous catalysis
rules.recommendation: Use particle size ratio dp < L/25 and dp < dc/10 where L is bed length and dc is bed diameter
rules.reasoning: Optimal particle size ensures proper mass transfer and avoids excessive pressure drops

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing catalyst performance in packed-bed reactors
rules.recommendation: Use smaller particles to facilitate better mass transfer due to increased surface area
rules.reasoning: Smaller particles increase surface area and improve mass transfer rates
rules.exceptions: Fine particles can cause unfavorable pressure drops

rules.condition: WHEN setting up fixed-bed reactors in laboratory
rules.recommendation: Fill chromatography column or stainless steel tube with catalyst particles and cap with sinters or filters, pump mobile phase upward to expel dead volumes
rules.reasoning: Upward flow helps eliminate dead volumes and ensures proper catalyst bed packing

rules.condition: WHEN catalyst deactivation occurs in fixed-bed reactors
rules.recommendation: Regenerate catalyst by calcinating under oxidizing atmosphere for coking issues
rules.reasoning: Calcination can restore catalyst activity by removing carbon deposits
rules.exceptions: Only applicable to certain types of catalyst poisoning

rules.condition: WHEN using solid-phase-supported reagents or inline scavengers in continuous flow
rules.recommendation: Use liquid chromatographic columns (such as those produced by Omnifit) instead of traditional fixed-bed reactors due to the large volume requirements
rules.reasoning: The volume of fixed-bed reactor required for solid-phase-supported reagents or inline scavengers - both employed in stoichiometric amounts, often in excess - needs to be quite large

rules.condition: WHEN designing tube-in-tube reactors for gas-liquid reactions
rules.recommendation: Use Teflon tube made of semipermeable AF-2400 membrane to introduce gaseous reactant through the walls into a concentric tube containing the reactant in liquid phase
rules.reasoning: Achieves rapid gas/liquid contact along the direction of the flow

rules.condition: WHEN operating tube-in-tube reactors
rules.recommendation: Match the rate of gas permeation across the membrane by the rate of its uptake by the liquid (through dissolution and consumption) to avoid formation of gas bubbles
rules.reasoning: Formation of gas bubbles must be avoided to maintain steady state in the reactor and ensure more even distribution of gaseous concentration along the reaction volume

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up falling film reactors
rules.recommendation: Scale out by stacking the reactor layers rather than increase the surface area
rules.reasoning: The geometry of a falling film reactor is characterized by its small thickness and large interfacial area, which inherently places an upper limit on its scalability
rules.quantitative: Reactors with dimensions of up to 1 m exist

rules.condition: WHEN performing highly exothermic biphasic gas-liquid reactions
rules.recommendation: Use falling film reactors to achieve good mass and heat transfer for good selectivity
rules.reasoning: The large interfacial area of falling film reactors provides excellent heat and mass transfer capabilities

rules.condition: WHEN performing photochemical reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Use falling film reactors to allow good transmission of photons across the glass and into the thin film
rules.reasoning: The large surface area of the falling film reactor is highly beneficial for photochemical reactions

rules.condition: WHEN using microwave reactors for single-mode operation
rules.recommendation: Use a frequency of 2.45 GHz (corresponding to wavelength of 12.2 cm) and ensure reactor diameter is narrower than the standing wave
rules.reasoning: This places an upper limit to the diameter of a reactor to achieve effective and homogeneous heating

rules.condition: WHEN needing to overcome diameter limitations in microwave reactors
rules.recommendation: Align several modes along the flow reactor tube to effectively extend the range of microwave irradiation
rules.reasoning: This overcomes the limitation imposed by the standing wave requirement for effective heating

rules.condition: WHEN designing large-scale continuous-flow microwave reactors
rules.recommendation: The largest available reactor can be operated up to 250°C and 30 bar, with productivity up to 2 ton h⁻¹
rules.reasoning: These are the current industrial scale limits for microwave flow reactors
rules.quantitative: Max temperature: 250°C, max pressure: 30 bar, max productivity: 2 ton h⁻¹

rules.condition: WHEN constructing simple continuous-flow photoreactors
rules.recommendation: Wrap transparent tubing around a light source
rules.reasoning: This simple design takes advantage of the large surface-area-to-volume ratio afforded by flow reactors for photon delivery

rules.condition: WHEN selecting tubing for compression fittings in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use rigid tubes made of metal or hard polymers (e.g., PEEK, nylon, Teflon, Kynar, or polyethylene) that can resist compression
rules.reasoning: These materials can resist the compression applied by the ferrule to form a tight seal

rules.condition: WHEN selecting fittings for medium pressure applications
rules.recommendation: Use 1/4-28 UNF threaded flat-bottomed connections for 1/8" and 1/16" tubing up to 20-30 bar pressure
rules.reasoning: These are standard for medium pressure applications and widely available
rules.quantitative: Pressure range: up to 20-30 bar, tubing sizes: 1/8", 1/16"

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow systems for high pressure operation
rules.recommendation: Use coned ports instead of flat-bottomed connections for pressures >30 bar
rules.reasoning: Coned ports are generally rated to higher pressures than similarly sized flat-bottomed connections

rules.condition: WHEN selecting standard fittings for HPLC systems
rules.recommendation: Use 10-32 UNF thread standard employed by many HPLC manufacturers
rules.reasoning: This is a common standard for high pressure liquid chromatography systems

rules.condition: WHEN selecting tubing sizes for flow systems
rules.recommendation: Standard HPLC accessories are available for tubes with 1/8", 1/16", and 1/32" OD
rules.reasoning: These are standard sizes with readily available fittings and accessories

rules.condition: WHEN threading compression nuts on fittings
rules.recommendation: Do not under- or over-tighten fittings, follow manufacturer's recommended procedures
rules.reasoning: Improper tightening can result in leaks, and installation of compression fittings differs between manufacturers and designs

rules.condition: WHEN using high-pressure flow reactor applications
rules.recommendation: Exercise extreme caution before intermixing and interchanging tube fittings from different manufacturers
rules.reasoning: Different manufacturers have dimensional and metallurgical differences that affect swaging behavior, making intermixing unsafe

rules.condition: WHEN using 1/4-28 flat-bottomed connections in low to medium pressure applications
rules.recommendation: Most 1/4-28 flat-bottomed connections are generally interchangeable when ferrule and nut are matched
rules.reasoning: Standardization allows interchangeability at lower pressures

rules.condition: WHEN reconnecting fittings multiple times
rules.recommendation: Always inspect fitting components for signs of damage and wear before reconnection, replace ferrules and/or fitting body if there are doubts about condition
rules.reasoning: Ensures leak-tight seal and prevents catastrophic failures

rules.condition: WHEN using polymer tubings in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Replace periodically to avoid catastrophic failures
rules.reasoning: Polymer tubings are generally mechanically less robust than SS tubes and can become brittle over time from exposure to chemical reagents, heat or UV light

rules.condition: WHEN selecting parts for 1/4-28 flat bottomed connections that need frequent disassembly
rules.recommendation: Select two-piece ferrules instead of single-piece ferrules
rules.reasoning: Single-piece 1/4-28 fitting ferrules tend to become stuck to the nut during installation and can result in twisting of tubing with potential to damage tubing wall. Two-piece ferrules have stainless steel locking ring allowing free rotation

rules.condition: WHEN installing pressure regulator in flow system
rules.recommendation: Install pressure regulator at start of system (between pump and reactor) with valve normally opened to regulate inlet pressure
rules.reasoning: Regulates inlet pressure to the system

rules.condition: WHEN installing BPR (Back Pressure Regulator) in flow system
rules.recommendation: Install BPR at end of flow process (usually before collection vessel) with valve normally closed to provide obstruction to flow and regulate upstream pressure
rules.reasoning: Keeps process under pressure and prevents outgassing and solvent evaporation

rules.condition: WHEN using piston pumps in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Install BPRs after pumps to work against pressure and help mechanical action of pump valves, prevent gravity feeding mobile phase through pump faster than intended
rules.reasoning: Helps mechanical action and prevents uncontrolled flow rates

rules.condition: WHEN monitoring flow system pressure
rules.recommendation: Install pressure sensor between pump and BPR, and include pressure sensor before end BPR to monitor pressure drop
rules.reasoning: Monitoring pump outlet helps troubleshoot pump faults like air locks and valve failures; pressure drop monitoring detects system blockages and leakages

rules.condition: WHEN selecting pump type for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Consider pressure range: Syringe (typically low, specialized can go very high), Peristaltic (low to medium, typically <10 bar), Piston (low to very high)
rules.reasoning: Different pump types have different pressure capabilities that affect reactor design
rules.exceptions: Specialized syringe pumps can achieve very high pressure

rules.condition: WHEN selecting pump type for flow rate requirements
rules.recommendation: Consider flow rate capabilities: Syringe (very low to medium), Peristaltic (medium to very high), Piston (low to high)
rules.reasoning: Different pump types have different flow rate capabilities

rules.condition: WHEN needing to dispense small volumes
rules.recommendation: Use syringe pumps which are good for small volumes down to picoliters
rules.reasoning: Syringe pumps excel at precise small volume dispensing

rules.condition: WHEN needing to move high volumes of liquid

rules.condition: WHEN performing cryogenic reactions in flow

rules.reasoning: Large surface-to-volume ratio allows effective dissipation of heat released, improving safety and control while reducing energy consumption
rules.quantitative: -30°C in flow vs -78°C in batch for lithiations

rules.condition: WHEN performing endergonic reactions requiring heating
rules.recommendation: Take advantage that only small reactor volume needs heating. Use back-pressure regulators to superheat low-boiling solvents at temperatures far exceeding atmospheric boiling point.
rules.reasoning: High surface-to-volume ratio enables effective heat input with small volumes, and back-pressure regulation allows use of superheated solvents
rules.quantitative: Can use solvents at >150°C when atmospheric bp is lower (see Table 4.1)
rules.exceptions: Prevents use of unfavorable high-boiling solvents like xylene, dichlorobenzene, NMP

rules.condition: WHEN using pressurized solvents in flow reactions
rules.recommendation: Use back-pressure regulators to enable low-boiling solvents at temperatures exceeding their atmospheric boiling points. This favors greener solvents over high-boiling alternatives.
rules.reasoning: Pressurization allows use of environmentally friendlier solvents that are easier to separate after reaction
rules.quantitative: MeCN: 82°C at 1 bar → 149°C at 10 bar → 183°C at 25 bar; THF: 66°C at 1 bar → 126°C at 10 bar → 157°C at 25 bar

rules.condition: WHEN starting a continuous flow process
rules.recommendation: Allow the flow system to reach steady state before collecting reaction product by pumping 1-2 reactor volumes of substrate solutions through the system.
rules.reasoning: Various variables (flow rate, temperature, pressure) affect the flow process and system needs stabilization to run continuously without fouling

rules.condition: WHEN monitoring flow processes
rules.recommendation: Use in-line analysis tools combined with suitable software to capture real-time data and report issues, allowing quick intervention if necessary.
rules.reasoning: Real-time monitoring enables immediate response to process deviations and maintains process control

rules.condition: WHEN dealing with solubility in flow processes
rules.recommendation: Pay close attention to solubility issues in flow processes, as heterogeneous catalysts in packed-bed reactors use 'contact time' highlighting substrate-reagent/catalyst contact importance.
rules.reasoning: Solubility issues can affect reaction efficiency and contact time between substrates and catalysts/reagents, especially in packed-bed systems

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow processes with insoluble starting materials or precipitation of products
rules.recommendation: Avoid inappropriately high concentration of solutions to prevent fouling within flow reactors due to blockages in microchannels, connectors, and back-pressure regulators
rules.reasoning: Insoluble starting materials or precipitation of products will lead to fouling within flow reactors due to blockages in microchannels, connectors, and back-pressure regulators
rules.exceptions: Solutions that avoid clogging and fouling are available to experienced experimentalists using peristaltic pumps, agitation, optimized solvents, and temperatures

rules.condition: WHEN starting flow chemistry process development
rules.recommendation: Conduct initial experiments in batch to evaluate solubility, reactivity, and stoichiometry for a given reaction before attempting this in continuous flow mode
rules.reasoning: Initial batch experiments help establish baseline conditions and understanding before translating to continuous flow

rules.condition: WHEN developing multistep flow sequences
rules.recommendation: Break down multistep sequences and integrate in-line purification aids as the process develops
rules.reasoning: This approach helps manage complexity and ensures compatibility between process steps

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow processes for downstream operations
rules.recommendation: Keep in mind all parameters that will affect downstream operations (quenching, additional transformations, purification, etc.) to ascertain compatibility of the intermediate material with subsequent reagents
rules.reasoning: Must consider compatibility of solvents, spent reagents, side-products with subsequent reagents as well as increasing dilution and flow rates

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing homogeneous flow reactions
rules.recommendation: Assess the solubility of all components (substrates, intermediates, products including side products) within the relevant temperature window
rules.reasoning: Solubility assessment is crucial for successful homogeneous flow reactions to avoid precipitation and blockages

rules.condition: WHEN performing difluorocyclopropanation reactions in flow

rules.condition: WHEN performing difluorocyclopropanation reactions in flow

rules.quantitative: One pump per reagent plus solvent line

rules.condition: WHEN calculating residence time in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Divide reactor volume (mL) by flow rate (mL min⁻¹) to determine residence time
rules.reasoning: Residence time is the total time solution is exposed to reaction conditions

rules.condition: WHEN adjusting residence time in flow
rules.recommendation: Increase flow rate to shorten residence time, decrease flow rate to lengthen residence time, or adjust reactor size if flow rates are constrained
rules.reasoning: Residence time is inversely proportional to flow rate for a given reactor volume
rules.quantitative: Inverse relationship between flow rate and residence time
rules.exceptions: May need to adjust reactor size if flow rates are dictated by other factors

rules.condition: WHEN working with flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Understand that there is direct correlation between reaction time and physical position, allowing identification of where reactive species are generated
rules.reasoning: Flow reactors provide spatial resolution of reaction progress, unlike batch reactors

rules.condition: WHEN tweaking stoichiometry between two reagent feeds in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Adjust relative flow rates by factors of 1.2X, 1.3X, or 1.4X while keeping one feed as limiting reagent
rules.reasoning: Small changes in flow rate ratios allow fine-tuning of stoichiometry without major system reconfiguration
rules.quantitative: Flow rate multipliers: 1.2X, 1.3X, 1.4X relative to baseline

rules.condition: WHEN changing total flow rate in a two-feed system with fixed reactor volume
rules.recommendation: Maintain stoichiometry by proportionally adjusting both feed rates (e.g., 1 mL/min each to 2 mL/min each)
rules.reasoning: Equal adjustment of both feeds preserves the molar ratio while changing overall residence time
rules.quantitative: Example: 1 mL/min + 1 mL/min = 2 mL/min total, then 2 mL/min + 2 mL/min = 4 mL/min total

rules.condition: WHEN adjusting both flow rates and initial concentrations simultaneously
rules.recommendation: Use different initial concentrations (1M vs 2M, 1M vs 4M, 1M vs 6M) combined with flow rate adjustments to achieve desired final concentrations
rules.reasoning: Allows independent control of both stoichiometry and final concentration in the mixed stream
rules.quantitative: Concentration ratios: 1M:2M, 1M:4M, 1M:6M with corresponding flow rate adjustments

rules.condition: WHEN calculating residence time in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Use the formula: Residence Time = Reactor Volume / Total Flow Rate
rules.reasoning: Residence time is inversely proportional to total flow rate for a fixed reactor volume
rules.quantitative: Examples: 10 mL reactor at 3 mL/min total = 3.33 min; at 6 mL/min total = 1.67 min
rules.exceptions: Does not account for dead volume or mixing effects

rules.condition: WHEN doubling total flow rate in a fixed volume reactor
rules.recommendation: Expect residence time to be cut in half
rules.reasoning: Residence time is inversely proportional to flow rate
rules.quantitative: Example: 3 mL/min total gives 3.33 min residence time, 6 mL/min gives 1.67 min

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow systems for cryogenic conditions requiring harness reactivity
rules.recommendation: Design systems to create pathways identical to batch reactions, controlling precise exposure time of reactive species
rules.reasoning: Flow systems can control time of exposure to reaction conditions more precisely than batch, minimizing unwanted side reactions
rules.exceptions: Limited by manual velocity of operator in batch systems

rules.condition: WHEN performing flash chemistry with highly reactive unstable species
rules.recommendation: Use microtube reactors with T-shaped micromixers, capable of very short residence times (0.8 s or less)
rules.reasoning: Flash chemistry relies on very fast reactions involving highly reactive species that cannot be harnessed by traditional methods
rules.quantitative: Residence time: 0.8 seconds for complete generation and trapping

rules.condition: WHEN performing monofunctionalization reactions like 1,2-dibromobenzene to produce specific intermediates
rules.recommendation: Use very short residence times to trap intermediates before undesired intramolecular elimination occurs
rules.reasoning: Residence time and temperature are key parameters for controlling reactivity of carbanions and preventing unwanted side reactions

rules.condition: WHEN performing lithiation reactions in flow with subsequent trapping
rules.recommendation: Use cooling baths at -78°C for T-shaped micromixers to control carbanion reactivity
rules.reasoning: Low temperature combined with short residence time prevents unwanted intramolecular elimination reactions

rules.condition: WHEN setting up organolithium generation and electrophile trapping in flow
rules.recommendation: Use two microtube reactors for lithiation and subsequent trapping, with two T-shaped micromixers submerged in cooling bath
rules.reasoning: Sequential reactor design allows controlled generation of organolithium followed by controlled electrophile addition
rules.quantitative: System uses two separate microtube reactors connected in series

rules.condition: WHEN working with flow systems for temperature control
rules.recommendation: Utilize the precise control over heat transfer and heat dissipation in narrow tubing/channels to achieve precise temperature control over each zone of the flow system
rules.reasoning: The intrinsic factor of narrow tubing and/or channels provides precise control over temperature applied to the reaction, which has important implications on selectivity and safe handling of exothermic reactions

rules.condition: WHEN working with solvents above their boiling point in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use pressurization to enable working with solvents above their boiling point, allowing temperature-dependent reactions to be accelerated from several hours to minutes
rules.reasoning: Flow systems can be easily pressurized, allowing one to work with solvents above their boiling point and accelerate temperature-dependent reactions
rules.quantitative: Reaction time reduction from several hours to minutes

rules.condition: WHEN performing temperature screening in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Apply rapid temperature ramps simultaneously to the entire flowing solution with no gradient, keeping all parameters identical except reaction temperature
rules.reasoning: Very good heat transfer in flow systems allows application of rapid temperature ramps with certainty that the same temperature is applied simultaneously to the entire flowing solution, enabling accurate dependence curves of reaction conversion and by-product formation

rules.condition: WHEN selecting tubing material for high temperature/pressure applications
rules.recommendation: Use stainless steel tubing for high temperature/pressure operations, as it can operate at high temperature/pressure conditions
rules.reasoning: The limiting factor to pressure that can be achieved is the strength of the tubing based on the material, and stainless steel can operate at high temperature/pressure

rules.condition: WHEN selecting pumps for high-pressure flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use high-performance liquid chromatography pumps for high pressure applications, avoid syringe pumps for high pressure
rules.reasoning: High-performance liquid chromatography pumps can work under high pressure, while syringe pumps generally cannot

rules.condition: WHEN working with reactions requiring temperatures above solvent boiling points
rules.recommendation: Apply 8-bar back-pressure regulator (BPR) at the end of the flow reactor to enable operation above solvent boiling point
rules.reasoning: This allows reactions requiring high temperatures (e.g., 110°C) to proceed even when using solvents with lower boiling points (e.g., 30% HCl)

rules.condition: WHEN applying pressure to flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use Back-Pressure Regulators (BPRs) to generate and hold backpressure, with spring mechanism cartridge-based BPRs being the simplest
rules.reasoning: Applying and maintaining pressure is safe and easy, prevents gas bubble formation with low boiling chemicals/solvents or gas-liberating reactions, increases gas solubility, and is beneficial when working with gaseous reagents

rules.condition: WHEN working with low boiling chemicals/solvents or reactions that liberate gases (N2 or CO2)
rules.recommendation: Work in a pressurized system to prevent gas bubble formation and increase gas solubility
rules.reasoning: Working in a pressurized system increases gas solubility and is highly beneficial when working with gaseous reagents

rules.condition: WHEN needing to vary pressure during ongoing processes
rules.recommendation: Use flexible BPRs that allow for variation of set pressure in manual or automated manner during ongoing process without stopping the stream
rules.reasoning: More flexible BPRs enable pressure variation without the need to stop the stream and dismantle the system
rules.exceptions: Simple cartridge-based BPRs require disassembly for pressure changes

rules.condition: WHEN designing multistep processes with longer residence times
rules.recommendation: Use slower flow rates and larger reactor volumes to accommodate longer residence times required for each step
rules.reasoning: Longer residence times require slower flow rates and larger reactor volumes, and flow rates in multistep processes depend on added reagent solution rates

rules.condition: WHEN designing multistep flow processes
rules.recommendation: Use specific sequence of reactors with customized volumes and/or very careful management of flow rate of every feed to achieve required residence times
rules.reasoning: Flow rate increases for each step due to continuous addition of reagents, requiring customized reactor volumes for each step
rules.quantitative: Example: 15 mL (15 min) → 20 mL (10 min) → 10 mL (3.33 min) reactors at 1 mL/min feed rate

rules.condition: WHEN developing multistep processes
rules.recommendation: Consider solvent compatibility between each step for both solubility and reactivity issues, identify multiple solvents to perform each step if possible, and minimize by-product formation
rules.reasoning: Solvent compatibility is critical between steps, and water-soluble by-products can be removed by in-line workup

rules.condition: WHEN one module in a multistep process is not tolerant of a solvent needed for another module
rules.recommendation: Remove the incompatible solvent from the reaction mixture before entering the intolerant module using in-line workup
rules.reasoning: Example: Module 3 (Michael addition) was not tolerant of methanol needed in Module 2, so methanol needed to be removed before entering Module 3
rules.quantitative: Example case: methanol removal between olefination and Michael addition modules

rules.condition: WHEN performing in-line workup between modules requiring solvent removal
rules.recommendation: Use toluene as the most efficient solvent for acidic in-line workup to remove methanol from reaction mixture
rules.reasoning: Toluene was found to be the most efficient solvent for acidic in-line workup between modules that removed methanol from the solution

rules.condition: WHEN transitioning from batch to flow chemistry processes
rules.recommendation: Shift thinking to consider not only which parameters need to be optimized, but also how they are screened, utilizing flow's advantages of high surface-to-volume ratios, precision in reagent delivery and reaction time, and ease of pressurization
rules.reasoning: Flow processes provide significantly more control over a procedure than comparable batch processes, often required to accelerate processes or make them possible

rules.condition: WHEN selecting Process Analytical Technology (PAT) tools for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Choose in-line PAT when the tool is located along the main stream, does not alter composition, keeps analyzed stream in main stream, and is amenable to automation
rules.reasoning: In-line PAT provides continuous monitoring without sample removal or composition changes

rules.condition: WHEN selecting Process Analytical Technology (PAT) tools for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Choose on-line PAT when the tool is located on a bypass line, does not alter composition, analyzed matter may reunite with main stream, and is amenable to automation
rules.reasoning: On-line PAT allows analysis without affecting main process stream

rules.condition: WHEN implementing in-line or on-line PAT sampling
rules.recommendation: Use a 'window' made from materials transparent to the analytical method in a flow-through cell that forms a single lumen with the reactor
rules.reasoning: This allows direct detection without requiring samples to physically exit the reactor
rules.exceptions: A dedicated flow cell may not be necessary if reactor material can accommodate the analytical technique (e.g., transparent PFA tube for Raman spectroscopy)

rules.condition: WHEN selecting window materials for spectroscopic analysis
rules.recommendation: Use glass for NMR spectroscopy or quartz for UV/vis spectroscopy
rules.reasoning: Material selection depends on the analytical method used

rules.condition: WHEN using pierceable membranes for at-line sampling
rules.recommendation: Consider the pressure tolerance of the pierceable membrane as it sets a limit on how much pressure the flow-through cell can withstand
rules.reasoning: The membrane must withstand process pressure while maintaining integrity

rules.condition: WHEN processes require high temperature with pierceable membranes
rules.recommendation: Evaluate chemical compatibility of the membrane at high temperatures
rules.reasoning: Chemical compatibility of the membrane is critical at elevated temperatures

rules.condition: WHEN using pierceable membranes for iterative sampling
rules.recommendation: Monitor membrane elasticity as pierceable membranes lose elasticity overtime, exposing reaction matter to open atmosphere
rules.reasoning: Loss of elasticity compromises sample isolation and process integrity

rules.condition: WHEN transporting samples from liquid-holding cups to analyzers
rules.recommendation: Avoid transporting samples too fast to prevent laminar flow in transport lines that could lead to random mixing between reactor sample and carrier solvent
rules.reasoning: Random mixing causes analyte concentration to change randomly

rules.condition: WHEN using sampling valves with configurable flow paths
rules.recommendation: Use custom etchings of configurable flow paths to mitigate issues from possible pressure differentials
rules.reasoning: Pressure differentials can cause operational issues when moving between load and inject configurations

rules.condition: WHEN using rotary sampling valves
rules.recommendation: Monitor that process pressure does not exceed the pressure threshold of the analytical device, as increasing physical dimension of configurable flow paths causes pressure tolerance to drop
rules.reasoning: Rising process pressure may exceed analytical device limits causing PAT tool failure

rules.condition: WHEN reactor throughput involves excessive amounts of solids
rules.recommendation: Use head-on filtration mechanisms to remove solid particulates before reactor samples enter analytics, or use multi-position valve sequences to maintain functional sampling valve
rules.reasoning: Solids can interfere with analytical measurements and sampling valve operation
rules.quantitative: Filter pore size: 20 μL mentioned in figure

rules.condition: WHEN performing sample preparation for integrated analysis
rules.recommendation: Include dilution, at the very least, to obtain meaningful results from downstream analyzer
rules.reasoning: Almost all forms of analysis require dilution for meaningful results

rules.condition: WHEN using tube-in-tube sampling tools for pharmaceutical manufacturing
rules.recommendation: Implement high sampling rates up to 0.1-6.0 μL min⁻¹ using push-pull capillary design where IPC sample is pulled into outer capillary while diluent is pushed through inner capillary
rules.reasoning: High sampling rates enable effective process monitoring, and the design prevents diluent contamination of the reaction vessel

rules.condition: WHEN choosing analytical tools for continuous production monitoring
rules.recommendation: Select analytical methods with short data acquisition time (fast scan rate) for processes sensitive to transient failures
rules.reasoning: Dynamic format of continuous production makes fast analytical methods essential for detecting transient failures

rules.condition: WHEN detecting small quantities of catalyst or reactive intermediates
rules.recommendation: Use high resolution analytical methods
rules.reasoning: High resolution methods are necessary to detect small quantities in reaction mixtures

rules.condition: WHEN working with optically opaque reaction matrices
rules.recommendation: Use attenuated total reflectance variants of IR and Raman techniques for reliable data acquisition
rules.reasoning: These optical methods can work with opaque matrices using flow-through cells

rules.condition: WHEN selecting flow cells for spectroscopic techniques
rules.recommendation: Select flow cells from designs fabricated specifically for the spectroscopic technique in question and ensure they cover a wide range of temperatures, pressures, and chemical compatibility
rules.reasoning: Proper flow cell selection ensures compatibility with process conditions and analytical requirements

rules.condition: WHEN selecting infrared spectroscopy as an integrated PAT tool for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use for nondestructive, in-line/on-line measurements with short acquisition time, suitable for detecting analytes in trace quantities
rules.reasoning: Infrared spectroscopy provides rapid, nondestructive analysis capabilities that are well-suited for real-time monitoring in flow systems
rules.quantitative: Short acquisition time, trace quantity detection capability
rules.exceptions: Does not provide structural information; analyte identification requires spectrum libraries

rules.condition: WHEN using infrared spectroscopy with separation tools in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Combine with integrated separation tool (e.g., at-line LC-IR) but note that inclusion of separation method extends analysis time
rules.reasoning: Separation extends analytical capabilities but increases total analysis time
rules.quantitative: Analysis time extension due to separation step
rules.exceptions: Not needed when reaction matrices are transparent and don't carry IR-active functional groups same as analytes

rules.condition: WHEN selecting Raman spectroscopy as an integrated PAT tool for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use for nondestructive, in-line/on-line measurements but avoid high-powered excitation lasers that degrade compounds
rules.reasoning: Raman spectroscopy is generally nondestructive but some high-powered excitation lasers can cause compound degradation
rules.quantitative: Short acquisition time for strong signals, long acquisition time for weak Raman signals
rules.exceptions: Weak Raman signals require long acquisition time

rules.condition: WHEN selecting UV/vis spectroscopy as an integrated PAT tool for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use for nondestructive, in-line/on-line measurements with short acquisition time, particularly suitable for detecting analytes in trace quantities
rules.reasoning: UV/vis spectroscopy provides rapid analysis with good sensitivity for trace detection
rules.quantitative: Short acquisition time, trace quantity detection capability
rules.exceptions: Does not provide structural information unless specific reagent and product chromophores don't overlap

rules.condition: WHEN using UV/vis spectroscopy with separation in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Integrate with separation tool (e.g., at-line LC-UV or LC-DAD) for enhanced analysis capabilities
rules.reasoning: Integrated LC allows orthogonal separation in multidimensional, multiattribute analysis from single injection
rules.exceptions: Inclusion of separation method extends analysis time

rules.condition: WHEN selecting NMR spectroscopy as an integrated PAT tool for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use for nondestructive, in-line/on-line measurements but expect longer acquisition times than IR, Raman, or UV techniques
rules.reasoning: NMR provides structural information but requires longer data acquisition times
rules.quantitative: Acquisition time in μL min⁻¹ range for modern high-field NMR instruments
rules.exceptions: Modern high-field NMR instruments can acquire sufficient data for relatively slow reactor throughput

rules.condition: WHEN using NMR spectroscopy for flow chemistry monitoring
rules.recommendation: Use deuterated solvents to minimize solvent-signal intensities; use nondeuterated solvents with solvent-signal suppression pulse sequence if no analyte signal appears at same region
rules.reasoning: Deuterated solvents reduce interference from solvent signals, while suppression techniques can be used when spectral overlap isn't an issue
rules.exceptions: When analyte signals don't overlap with solvent signals in the spectrum

rules.condition: WHEN using multi-nuclear NMR for flow chemistry PAT
rules.recommendation: Use naturally abundant nuclei (¹H, ¹⁹F, ¹³C, ²⁹Si) to monitor specific analytes bearing these atoms without interference from reaction matrices
rules.reasoning: Multi-nuclear NMR allows selective monitoring of compounds containing specific atoms while avoiding matrix interference
rules.quantitative: Specific nuclei: ¹H, ¹⁹F, ¹³C, ²⁹Si
rules.exceptions: Various multinuclear NMR pulse techniques like HSQC and HMBC have long data acquisition times

rules.condition: WHEN selecting mass spectrometry as an integrated PAT tool for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use for short acquisition time when coupled as stand-alone at-line tool, suitable for detecting analytes in trace quantities
rules.reasoning: Mass spectrometry provides rapid analysis with excellent sensitivity and structural information capabilities
rules.quantitative: Short acquisition time, trace quantity detection, molecular ion level structural information

rules.condition: WHEN using mass spectrometry with separation in flow chemistry

rules.reasoning: Different ionization methods (ESI, APCI, EI, CI) allow coupling with appropriate separation tools and matching to critical quality attributes
rules.quantitative: Various ionization modes available: ESI, APCI, EI, CI

rules.condition: WHEN using LC or GC separation techniques in flow chemistry PAT

rules.condition: WHEN telescoping multiple reaction steps in continuous flow sequence

rules.condition: WHEN performing Aldol condensation reactions in flow

rules.condition: WHEN designing membrane-based liquid-liquid separators

rules.reasoning: Hydrophobic membranes selectively allow organic phases to pass through while blocking aqueous phases based on wetting properties

rules.condition: WHEN operating membrane separators
rules.recommendation: Ensure pressure drop through membrane (ΔPmem) is less than capillary pressure (ΔPc) to retain aqueous phase: ΔPc > ΔPmem
rules.reasoning: If membrane pressure drop exceeds capillary pressure, the aqueous phase cannot be retained and will breakthrough the membrane
rules.quantitative: P₁ = ΔPmem + P₂; ΔPc > ΔPmem where P₁ is retentate pressure, P₂ is permeate pressure

rules.condition: WHEN calculating capillary pressure for membrane separators
rules.recommendation: Use Young-Laplace equation to estimate capillary pressure
rules.reasoning: Capillary pressure determines the breakthrough pressure for the non-wetting phase through the membrane
rules.quantitative: Capillary pressure estimated by Young-Laplace equation

rules.condition: WHEN using hydrophobic PTFE membranes for continuous aqueous-organic separation
rules.recommendation: Calculate capillary pressure using ΔPc = (2γ/Rmem)cosθ where γ is interfacial tension, Rmem is pore size, and θ is wetting angle
rules.reasoning: The capillary pressure determines the minimum pressure difference needed to push the organic phase through the membrane

rules.condition: WHEN operating porous membrane-based separators
rules.recommendation: Ensure ΔPper < ΔPmem < ΔPc where ΔPper is permeate pressure drop, ΔPmem is membrane pressure difference, and ΔPc is capillary pressure
rules.reasoning: The organic phase needs to travel through the membrane, requiring its pressure drop to be less than the membrane pressure difference, which must be less than the capillary pressure

rules.condition: WHEN ΔPmem falls below ΔPper in membrane separation
rules.recommendation: Expect retention - the permeate phase will be retained and not permeated
rules.reasoning: Insufficient pressure difference prevents the organic phase from passing through the membrane

rules.condition: WHEN ΔPmem becomes higher than ΔPc in membrane separation
rules.recommendation: Expect breakthrough - some retentate phase will permeate through the membrane
rules.reasoning: Excessive pressure difference overcomes the capillary pressure, causing unwanted phase crossover

rules.condition: WHEN designing self-tuning pressure elements for membrane separators
rules.recommendation: Design diaphragm material and structure so that ΔPper < ΔPdia < ΔPc
rules.reasoning: The diaphragm tension must provide pressure difference within the operating range to ensure complete liquid-liquid separation

rules.condition: WHEN using self-tuning pressure elements
rules.recommendation: Set ΔPdia = ΔPmem where ΔPdia = ΔP1 - ΔP2 depends on diaphragm tension
rules.reasoning: The diaphragm acts as a self-regulating pressure control element that automatically maintains proper separation conditions

rules.condition: WHEN operating multistage countercurrent extraction with membrane separators

rules.quantitative: Qset > Qret_out,i for each stage i
rules.exceptions: When Qset < Qret_out,i, breakthrough occurs due to pressure controller failure

rules.condition: WHEN designing membrane separators for flow chemistry

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up exothermic reactions (ΔrHm < 0)

rules.condition: WHEN calculating heat removal requirements

rules.reasoning: Heat removal rate is proportional to area and temperature difference, inversely proportional to wall thickness and thermal resistance

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up conventional reactors from 10 to 2,000 mL

rules.reasoning: Specific area for heat transfer decreases by 83% when volume increases from 10 to 2,000 mL, making heat removal inadequate

rules.condition: WHEN using micro- and mesofluidic reactors

rules.condition: WHEN designing cylindrical pressure containers (tubes)
